entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms the 1995 NPT Middle East resolution and supports creating a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD. It welcomes the ongoing annual Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free Zone (launched in 2018), participates as an observer, and urges engagement by all regional states and all nuclear-weapon states.
entries.quote: We reaffirm the significance and relevance of the 1995 resolution on the Middle East adopted at the NPT Review and Extension Conference. As one of its co-sponsors, we support the efforts to establish a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the region. We welcome the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched in 2018 and in which Russia takes part as an observer. It is important that all the countries of the region and all nuclear-weapon States engaged in its work.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports convening a UN-led conference to establish a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, backing UNGA decision 73/546 as a concrete step forward. It blames the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada for obstructing progress and frames the initiative as aligned with the UN’s peace and security mandate.
entries.quote: 7. More than 20 years without any tangible progress in implementing the 1995 resolution has predictably led to growing discontent among the Arab countries of the region. In view of the stalemate, the League of Arab States initiated returning the WMD-free zone issue to “inside the walls” of the United Nations and holding of a relevant conference, facilitated by the Secretary-General of the United Nations. The Russian Federation supported that step, based primarily on the mandate of the United Nations to maintain international peace and security, but also in view of the blatant sabotage of that process by Washington, London and Ottawa, which had aligned itself with them. As a result, the General Assembly adopted decision 73/546 of 23 December 2018 on convening a United Nations conference on the establishment of WMD-free zone in the Middle East, marking the first practical step towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone since 1974.

entries.position_summary: Russia expresses consistent and strong support for establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, calling it a high foreign policy priority. It views the zone as an effective means to strengthen regional security and the broader non-proliferation regime, benefiting all states in and beyond the region.
entries.quote: STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Delegation at the First Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons on the Establishment of a Zone Free of Nuclear and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction in the Middle East (Vienna, 7 August 2023) Mr. Chair, The Russian Federation has consistently supported the idea of establishing a zone free of nuclear and other weapons of mass destruction (a WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. This issue has traditionally been high on the list of our foreign policy priorities, since we regard it as an effective way of strengthening regional security and the international non-proliferation regime. The creation of such a zone would be in the interests of all without exception States in the Middle East and beyond.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the establishment of nuclear-weapon-free zones under Article VII of the NPT, considering them a crucial component of the non-proliferation regime.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, The establishment of nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs) under Article VII of the NPT is also a crucial element of the non-proliferation regime.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the Southeast Asia Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone and is ready to consult with member states and sign the Protocol to the Bangkok Treaty at any time.
entries.quote: We are open to consultations with the States parties to the Treaty on the Southeast Asia Nuclear Weapon-Free Zone with a view to signing the Protocol to the Bangkok Treaty as soon as possible. We are ready to sign such a Protocol at any time.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a WMD-free (and historically nuclear-weapon-free) zone in the Middle East, viewing it as beneficial for regional and global security. It assesses that near-term implementation is unlikely under current conditions but commits to supporting the process as much as possible.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, During the half a century that have passed since the UN General Assembly adopted the first resolution on a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the Middle East in 1974, a great deal of effort has been made to make a ME WMDFZ reality. Such a zone would considerably strengthen international peace and security both within the region and beyond. Unfortunately, in the current circumstances the chances of practical implementation of this initiative in the near future are slim. We see our task here in supporting this process as much as possible.

entries.position_summary: Russia emphasizes that it has ratified the protocols to the NWFZ treaties in Latin America and the Caribbean, the South Pacific, Africa, and Central Asia, thereby providing legally binding negative security assurances against the threat or use of nuclear weapons to over 100 States. It adds that its accessions include clarifying reservations, which it claims do not affect States that adhere to the letter and spirit of the NWFZ arrangements in good faith.
entries.quote: Legally binding security assurances have already been granted to the countries that have established nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs) in different parts of the world. Russia has ratified the protocols to the existing treaties establishing NWFZs in Latin America and the Caribbean (Treaty of Tlatelolco), the South Pacific (Treaty of Rarotonga), Africa (Treaty of Pelindaba), and Central Asia (Semipalatinsk Treaty), thus providing security assurances against the threat or use of nuclear weapons to over one hundred States parties to such zones. While acceding to such protocols, Russia traditionally makes reservations that serve the purposes of clarity and do not affect the interests of States following the "letter and spirit" of NWFZ arrangements in good faith.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports expanding Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zones and highlights that it has ratified all signed protocols providing negative security assurances to these zones. It states its reservations to these protocols are clarifications that do not undermine the interests of states adhering to NWFZ arrangements in good faith.
entries.quote: Madam Chair, Nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs), directly envisaged by Article VII of the NPT, play an important role in ensuring the non-proliferation of nuclear weapons. Russia has always supported efforts to expand the geographic scope of such zones. It has ratified all the protocols on negative security assurances to the treaties establishing NWFZs that it has signed. Russia's reservations to the mentioned protocols serve the purposes of clarity and do not affect the interests of States who follow the "letter and spirit" of NWFZ arrangements in good faith.

entries.position_summary: Russia strongly supports establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone as envisaged by the 1995 NPT resolution, seeing it as vital for regional stability and non-proliferation. It participates as an observer in the annual UN conference on the zone and urges engagement by all invited states.
entries.quote: Russia has consistently supported efforts to establish a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference. Such a zone would serve as an effective means of strengthening regional stability and the nuclear non-proliferation regime for the benefit of all the States of the Middle East without exception. As an observer State, Russia contributes to the annual UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East WMD-free zone. It is vitally important to ensure the engagement of all the invited States in the work of the Conference.

entries.position_summary: The country marks the 50th anniversary of UNGA resolution 3263 and expresses strong support for establishing a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the Middle East, noting sustained efforts via the NPT, the UN, and the IAEA. It argues that achieving this goal would significantly enhance regional and global peace and security.
entries.quote: 1. December 2024 marked 50 years since the adoption by the General Assembly, at the initiative of Iran and Egypt, of resolution 3263 (XXIX) entitled “Establishment of a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the region of the Middle East”. Over the past half- century, many efforts have been made by the countries of the region, both through the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and at the United Nations and the International Atomic Energy Agency, to achieve the noble goal of realizing this initiative, the implementation of which would seriously enhance international peace and security not only in the Middle East but also beyond.

entries.position_summary: The country strongly supports establishing nuclear-weapon-free zones under Article VII of the NPT and advocates that nuclear-weapon states provide legally binding security assurances to NWFZ parties. It views these steps as strengthening the non-proliferation regime and enhancing regional security and stability.
entries.quote: Nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs) are an essential component of the non- proliferation regime. Establishing such zones is envisaged by Article VII of the NPT. We consider establishing such zones and nuclear-weapon States' signing legally binding protocols on security assurances to the parties to such zones as a meaningful factor strengthening the non-proliferation regime and regional security and stability.

entries.position_summary: Russia underscores the importance of NWFZs under Article VII and supports legally binding protocols by nuclear-weapon states that provide security assurances to NWFZ parties. It states it has signed and ratified the protocols to the Tlatelolco, Rarotonga, Pelindaba, and Semipalatinsk treaties, thereby extending negative security assurances to over 100 States.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, Nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs) are a pivotal component of the non- proliferation regime. Establishing such zones is envisaged by Article VII of the NPT. We consider establishing such zones and nuclear-weapons States' signing legally binding protocols on security assurances to the parties to such zones as a meaningful factor strengthening international security and stability. Russia has signed and ratified the protocols to the existing treaties establishing NWFZs in Latin America and the Caribbean (Treaty of Tlatelolco), the South Pacific (Treaty of Rarotonga), Africa (Treaty of Pelindaba), Central Asia (Semipalatinsk Treaty), thus providing security assurances against the threat or use of nuclear weapons to over one hundred States parties to such zones.

entries.position_summary: Russia states it has acceded to all protocols of existing Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone treaties (Tlatelolco, Rarotonga, Pelindaba, Semipalatinsk), affirming support for NWFZs. Through these accessions, it provides negative security assurances—guarantees against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons—to over a hundred NWFZ states.
entries.quote: Russia has fully acceded to all protocols to the existing treaties on NWFZs – the Tlatelolco, Rarotonga, Pelindaba and Semipalatinsk Treaties - thus providing over a hundred NWFZ States with guarantees against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: Russia affirms it has signed and ratified the protocols to the Tlatelolco, Rarotonga, Pelindaba, and Semipalatinsk treaties, thereby extending legally binding negative security assurances to over 100 NWFZ states. This indicates support for NWFZ regimes and assurances against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons.
entries.quote: Russia has signed and ratified protocols to the existing Treaties establishing NWFZs in Latin America and the Caribbean (Treaty of Tlatelolco), in the South Pacific (Treaty of Rarotonga), in Africa (Treaty of Pelindaba), and in Central Asia (Treaty of Semipalatinsk), thus providing over a hundred NWFZ States with assurances against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East and argues it would enhance regional and global stability. It consistently advocates for this zone as a contribution to regional security.
entries.quote: Russia is a consistent supporter of the establishment of a zone free of nuclear and other types of WMD (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. We are convinced that its creation will contribute to the stability of the Middle East region and globally.

entries.position_summary: Russia affirms it has signed and ratified protocols to NWFZ treaties in Latin America/Caribbean, South Pacific, Africa, and Central Asia, thereby extending negative security assurances to their parties. It adds that its accessions include reservations for clarity that do not affect states complying with NWFZ obligations in good faith.
entries.quote: Russia has signed and ratified the protocols to the existing treaties establishing NWFZs in Latin America and the Caribbean (Treaty of Tlatelolco), the South Pacific (Treaty of Rarotonga), Africa (Treaty of Pelindaba), Central Asia (Semipalatinsk Treaty), thus providing security assurances against the threat or use of nuclear weapons to over one hundred States parties to such zones. While acceding to such protocols, Russia traditionally makes reservations that serve the purposes of clarity and do not affect the interests of States following the "letter and spirit" of NWFZ arrangements in good faith.

entries.position_summary: The country supports establishing a Middle East nuclear-weapon-free zone in line with SSOD I and urges states in the region to refrain from nuclear weapons-related activities and place all nuclear activities under IAEA safeguards pending its creation. It contends that Israel’s failure to meet these prerequisites has obstructed the start of substantive negotiations.
entries.quote: 2. The initiative to establish a nuclear-weapon-free zone (NWFZ) was further confirmed by consensus at the tenth special session of the General Assembly (A/S-10/4) in 1978. Moreover, the States Members of the United Nations, within the framework of the then programme of action on disarmament, proposed that “[p]ending the establishment of such a zone in the region, States of the region should … refrain on a reciprocal basis from producing, acquiring or in any other way possessing nuclear weapons and nuclear explosive devices and from permitting the stationing of nuclear weapons on their territory by any third party, and agree to place all their nuclear activities under International Atomic Energy Agency safeguards”.1 Unfortunately, this key requirement of the General Assembly for the start of substantive negotiations has not yet been met by Israel, which has largely predetermined further difficulties in launching the negotiation process.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the United States for signing but not ratifying the protocols to the Rarotonga, Pelindaba, and Semipalatinsk Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone treaties (while excepting Tlatelolko), indicating reluctance to undertake legally binding commitments. It argues that this undermines efforts to achieve a universal agreement on negative security assurances.
entries.quote: 2 The United States, on the contrary, have signed but have never ratified the protocols to the Treaties of Rarotonga, Pelindaba and Semipalatinsk, making an exception exclusively for the Treaty of Tlatelolko, which established the first nuclear-weapon-free zone. The US lack of readiness to assume legal obligations may hamper the elaboration and adoption of a universal agreement on NSA.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East and will continue contributing as an observer to the UN conference on this initiative. It underscores this as a longstanding non-proliferation priority and references the October 23, 2024 BRICS Kazan declaration in support.
entries.quote: 14. The Russian Federation will continue, commensurate with its role as an observer at the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone, to contribute to this crucial task in any way possible. Establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East has consistently been one of our country’s foreign policy priorities in the area of non-proliferation. This was also set out in the BRICS Kazan declaration entitled “Strengthening multilateralism for just global development and security”, adopted on 23 October 2024.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports the process to establish a Middle East WMD-free zone by participating as an observer, welcomes other NWS observers, and stresses the importance of engagement by the IAEA Secretariat, OPCW, and the BWC Implementation Support Unit.
entries.quote: Russia participates as an observer in the UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction convened in accordance with the UN General Assembly decision 73/546 of 23 December 2018. We welcome the role and contribution of other observers: China, the United Kingdom and France. We believe it important the IAEA Secretariat, the OPCW and the BWC Implementation Support Unit have engaged in this work.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes Israel for refusing to participate in the UN conference established by GA decision 73/546 to create a Middle East WMD-free zone. It argues Israel would be protected by consensus rules and could join even as an observer, and faults Israel for isolating itself from key regional security talks.
entries.quote: 10. Regrettably, despite the inclusive nature of General Assembly decision 73/546, Israel continues to refuse to join the process. However, it should be understood that any participant in the Conference is well protected by the rule of consensus on any topics under consideration. Nothing would appear to prevent Tel Aviv from participating in the forum, even initially as an observer. Instead, the Israelis prefer to isolate themselves from the most important Middle East negotiations inextricably linked to regional security, and the only ones involving all States in the region.

entries.position_summary: The country laments that only one Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone has all its instruments in force and criticizes the United States for not ratifying protocols to the remaining NWFZ treaties. It urges Washington to ratify these protocols, thereby completing the legal framework and associated negative security assurances.
entries.quote: We deplore that to date, only one NWFZ has a full set of international instruments in force. I would like to stress that the protocols to the remaining NWFZ treaties have not been ratified by the United States. We call on Washington to

entries.position_summary: Russia considers the 1995 Middle East WMD-free zone resolution an integral part of the 1995 NPT package, supports the UNGA-launched annual conference process, and participates as an observer. It stresses that the process must include all Middle Eastern states and urges Israel and the United States to engage in the conference and open discussion.
entries.quote: The issue of establishing a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference, has always played special role in the NPT context. We view this resolution as an integral element of the 1995 NPT agreements. Russia welcomes the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched by the UN General Assembly in 2018 and in which our country takes part as an observer. A series of substantive discussions has already taken place, and a number of specific decisions has been made. Certainly, this process can only be meaningful if it involves all Middle East countries excepting no one. We once again call on Israel and the United States not to disregard the Conference and take this opportunity to express their opinion and engage in an open discussion on strengthening security in the Middle East.

entries.position_summary: Supports convening the UN Conference on establishing a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD as a way to advance progress despite NPT deadlock, emphasizing regional ownership by Middle East states. The statement portrays the conference as an effective alternative path to move the process forward.
entries.quote: However, it then became clear that, given the deadlock on the NPT platform, the idea of convening a UN Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction could open an additional window of opportunity to advance the process. And so it turned out to be. In practice, Middle East States took the matter of creating a WMD-free zone into their own hands.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the United States for refusing to participate in the UN Conference on establishing a Middle East WMD-free zone and for neglecting its obligations as a co-sponsor of the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East. It accuses Washington of sabotaging efforts to create the zone despite claiming leadership in nonproliferation.
entries.quote: We regret to note that the United States, by its non-participation in the Conference, continues to refrain from its responsibilities as one of the co-sponsors of the 1995 Resolution on the Middle East. We see no real reason for Washington, which, moreover, is constantly trying to claim in words to be a leader in non- proliferation, to further sabotage the meetings aimed at establishing the Middle East WMD-free zone.

entries.position_summary: Russia notes progress toward defining a Middle East WMD-free zone treaty, highlights the need for expert support on its nuclear aspects, and suggests leveraging Vienna’s technical expertise and institutions. It offers its good offices in an observer capacity if regional states request them.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, Following the results of the three sessions of the United Nations Conference, it is clear that the participants are gradually approaching the discussion of the concrete outlines of the future Treaty on the Middle East WMD-free zone. In this connection, there is a real demand from the States of the region for expert support for the New York discussions, first and foremost with regard to the nuclear aspects of the future zone. We believe that the potential of other United Nations host cities, in particular Vienna, which has the necessary expertise, where discussions take place in a more technical and depoliticized manner and where specialized international organizations are also located, could be of practical benefit in this matter. Within its observer status, the Russian side is ready to provide its good offices if States of the region deem them necessary.

entries.position_summary: The country welcomes the annual conference process launched in 2018 to establish a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD, values its open and inclusive dialogue, and notes Russia’s active participation as an observer. It expresses support for continued engagement in this process.
entries.quote: We welcome the 2018-launched process of annual sessions of the Conference on the Establishment of a Middle East Zone Free of Nuclear Weapons and Other Weapons, in which Russia takes an active part as an observer. It is important that an open and inclusive dialogue has been initiated and that States of

entries.position_summary: The country laments that only one nuclear-weapon-free zone treaty has been fully recognized under international law and urges relevant states to complete domestic ratification procedures to strengthen NWFZ status across willing regions.
entries.quote: We regret that to date only one treaty on the establishment of a NWFZ has been fully recognized by international law. We call on the States concerned to complete necessary domestic procedures and thereby strengthen the nuclear- weapon-free status of so many regions of our planet that have expressed their willingness.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes Australia’s acquisition of nuclear-powered submarines as formally compatible with the Rarotonga Treaty but creating risks by introducing nuclear-weapon-state military infrastructure that could enable future nuclear weapon deployment. It argues that, given these risks, the provision of security assurances to Australia as a NWFZ party should be reconsidered.
entries.quote: Australia's actions as a party to the South Pacific Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone Treaty (Rarotonga Treaty) are highly controversial. Possession of nuclear submarines is not formally prohibited by this Treaty, and, formally, Australia does not intend to acquire nuclear weapons, however, the creation of military infrastructure of nuclear-weapon States dramatically increases the risk of such weapons being deployed there one day, which makes it necessary to take another look at the advisability of providing security assurances to Australia as a NWFZ State.

entries.position_summary: The statement supports the establishment and effective functioning of nuclear-weapon-free zones under Article VII as important for strengthening the non-proliferation regime. It advocates that nuclear-weapon states accede to NWFZ treaty protocols as the best way to provide legally binding security assurances to states in those zones.
entries.quote: The establishment and functioning of nuclear-weapon-free zones (NWFZs) envisaged by Article VII of the NPT is an important element of strengthening the nuclear non-proliferation regime. Nuclear-weapon States' accession to the relevant protocols to the treaties establishing such zones is the best way to provide the

entries.position_summary: Russia underscores its support for Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zones, stating it has ratified all signed NWFZ protocols and thereby provided legally binding negative security assurances to over 100 states, with reservations claimed to be purely clarificatory. It further asserts that NWFZ members must not only refrain from deploying nuclear weapons but also avoid establishing infrastructure enabling their use.
entries.quote: 4 members of the NWFZs with legally binding assurances against the use or threat of use of nuclear weapons against them. Russia has ratified all the protocols to treaties establishing nuclear-weapon-free zones that is signed and has thus provided negative security assurances to over one hundred countries. Russia's reservations to such instruments serve the purposes of clarity and do not affect the interests of States abiding by NWFZ arrangements in good faith. It should be understood that the members of the NWFZs are not merely expected not to deploy nuclear weapons in their territories, they are not to establish infrastructure for the use of such weapons.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the AUKUS arrangement, warning it could enable nuclear-weapon States to build military infrastructure and potentially deploy nuclear weapons on the territory of non-nuclear-weapon States party to the Treaty of Rarotonga. It argues this risk undermines the South Pacific Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone and validates its prior reservations.
entries.quote: Such clarifying reservations have been wholly justified, as the situation around the United States', United Kingdom's and Australia's AUKUS deal has demonstrated: one cannot exclude the possibility that as a result of this partnership nuclear-weapon States create their military infrastructure and, in the future, nuclear weapons in the territory of non-nuclear-weapon States parties to the Treaty of Rarotonga.

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes the United States for avoiding the Middle East WMD-free zone conference and for applying double standards to regional non-proliferation concerns. It argues this undermines efforts toward a comprehensive regional solution and weakens international peace and security, implying support for advancing the Middle East WMDFZ process.
entries.quote: The efforts of Washington to avoid the Conference do significant damage to the interests of nuclear non-proliferation. It is unacceptable to completely disregard the nuclear non-proliferation concerns of some countries in the region while exaggerating those same concerns for others. Such an approach only distances us from achieving a comprehensive solution to the problems of the Middle East and hinders the strengthening of international peace and security in the region, as envisioned in the Charter of the United Nations.

entries.position_summary: The country supports the Conference’s consensus-only decision-making and the creation of an intersessional working committee, seeing these modalities as enabling deeper substantive discussion and building trust among regional states. It views these steps as constructive for advancing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East.
entries.quote: Crucially, the participants have agreed that all decisions of the Conference, whether procedural or substantive, will be taken solely by consensus. It is also significant that the participating States have established an intersessional working committee, which allows for a more substantive and in-depth discussion of issues related to the mandate of the Conference, which in turn plays an important role in building trust among the countries of the region.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes U.S. extended nuclear deterrence and joint nuclear planning with the ROK (and possible involvement of Japan) as destabilizing and fueling an arms race in the Asia-Pacific. It opposes U.S. deployment and transfer of nuclear-capable delivery systems to allies—especially submarines for Australia—arguing this raises compliance concerns under the NPT and the Treaty of Rarotonga.
entries.quote: Washington's steps to replicate such schemes in other parts of the world where the United States already practices its so-called "extended nuclear deterrence," also have extremely negative implications for regional and global security. In particular, the United States' and Republic of Korea's joint "nuclear planning" activities accompanied by calls to involve Japan in the process, create considerable tension in the Asia-Pacific and spur arms race. These tensions are fuelled by Washington's active steps to deploy its strategic platforms in the region, including nuclear delivery vehicles, and plans to transfer systems that could carry nuclear weapons, to their allies. In particular, the United States intends to transfer to Australia submarines designed, among other things, to carry nuclear cruise missiles that are under development. This poses a number of serious questions in the context of both the NPT and the Treaty of Rarotonga.

entries.position_summary: The country condemns AUKUS, arguing it establishes nuclear-weapons-state infrastructure in a non-nuclear-weapon, NWFZ country, creating the potential for future nuclear weapon deployment. It views this as destabilizing and detrimental to nuclear disarmament efforts in the Asia-Pacific.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, We disapprove of the AUKUS trilateral partnership. It creates a fundamentally new geopolitical situation in the Asia-Pacific region. Within the partnership, an infrastructure of nuclear-weapon States, which can later be used to deploy nuclear weapons, is being established in a non-nuclear-weapon State that is also a NWFZ country. This produces an additional factor of instability that undermines nuclear disarmament efforts.

entries.position_summary: The country contends that reservations to protocols of Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zones are justified, citing AUKUS as a case that could allow nuclear-weapon states to establish military infrastructure in a non-nuclear-weapon state party to the Treaty of Rarotonga. It implies concern that naval nuclear propulsion cooperation under AUKUS risks undermining the South Pacific NWFZ.
entries.quote: Such reservations to the protocols to the treaties establishing nuclear-weapon- free zones have been wholly justified, as the situation around the United States', United Kingdom's and Australia's AUKUS deal has demonstrated, as one cannot exclude the possibility that as a result of such partnership nuclear-weapon States create their military infrastructure in the territory of a non-nuclear-weapon State party to the Treaty of Rarotonga.

entries.position_summary: Supports resolving remaining issues related to the protocol to the Bangkok Treaty (SEANWFZ) and expresses readiness to engage to finalize it, enabling its conclusion and associated assurances.
entries.quote: We hope for an early resolution of all outstanding issues arising from the signing of the relevant protocol to the Bangkok Treaty. We are ready for that.

entries.position_summary: Russia acknowledges making reservations when signing instruments related to Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zones but asserts these do not affect states that fully comply with NWFZ obligations. It frames such reservations as safeguards to ensure NWFZ parties adhere to their commitments, implicitly conditioning the scope of security assurances on compliance.
entries.quote: In this regard, I would like to note once again that Russia, like most other nuclear powers, traditionally makes a number of reservations when signing such documents. But it should be clearly understood that our reservations in no way affect the interests of States that intend to strictly adhere to their obligations under a relevant treaty establishing a NWFZ. They are merely a tool to ensure that NWFZ States comply with the provisions of the agreements they concluded.

entries.position_summary: The country urges states to complete domestic procedures to formalize and strengthen nuclear-weapon-free zones in regions that have declared intent to establish them. It supports expanding and consolidating NWFZs globally.
entries.quote: 4 complete the necessary internal procedures and thus strengthen the nuclear-weapon- free status of the impressive number of our planet's regions that expressed such intention.
